Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Mizokuihime-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kōriyama)|ja|湯泉神社 (郡山市)}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}, {{ill|Kōriyama|ja|郡山市}}, {{ill|Bandai-Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉}}) - Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, among others. Divinity of hot springs brought from Izu by the {{ill|Azumi Itō Clan|ja|安積伊東氏}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Iwaki)|ja|温泉神社 (いわき市)}} (Fukushima Prefecture, {{ill|Iwaki|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Iwaki Yumoto Onsen|ja|いわき湯本温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|事代主命}}, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōtawara)|ja|温泉神社 (大田原市中野内)}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Kurobane Onsen|ja|黒羽温泉}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nasushiobara)|ja|温泉神社 (那須塩原市湯本塩原)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasushiobara|ja|那須塩原市}}, {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉郷}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Nasu Onsen Shrine|ja|那須温泉神社}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡}}, {{ill|Nasu|ja|那須町}}, {{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷}}) - Shikinai-sha. Famous for the {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}. The "Nasu Yumoto Daijingū" mentioned in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}} is said to refer to this shrine. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nikkō)|ja|温泉神社 (日光市)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日光市}}, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}) * {{ill|Yumae Shrine|ja|湯前神社}} ({{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海市}}, {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Izuyama Shrine|ja|射山神社}} ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, {{ill|Tsu|ja|津市}}, {{ill|Sakakibara Onsen|ja|榊原温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kobe)|ja|湯泉神社}} ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市}}, {{ill|Kita-ku (Kobe)|ja|北区}}, {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Sometimes referred to as "Onsen Shrine." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and Kumano-Kusumi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Shisho Shrine (Toyooka)|ja|四所神社 (豊岡市)}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Toyooka|ja|豊岡市}}, {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}}) - Enshrines Yuyama-no-Kami (湯山主神) and others. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Yakami-hime-no-Mikoto, Miino-Kami, and Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Tamatsukuri Shrine|ja|玉作湯神社}} ({{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市}}, {{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|ja|玉造温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Kushiakarutama-no-Kami, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yu Shrine (Dōgo Onsen)|ja|湯神社}} ({{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}, {{ill|Matsuyama|ja|松山市}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社}}. Enshrines Amenominakanushi-no-Kami, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}) - Founded during the Taishō period. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. Currently merged into {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu Tetsuwana)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, {{ill|Tetsuwana Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}}) * {{ill|Hinokuma Hime Shrine|ja|火男火売神社}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, Tetsuwana Onsen) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Hinokagutsuchi-no-Mikoto and Hiyakehayahime-no-Mikoto. It is said that {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍}} Shōnin was inspired by this shrine, called Tsurumi Gongen, to create a stone steam bath (mushi-yu). "Hayami no Yu," a hot spring flowing from the base of the sacred Tsurumidake mountain, is believed to have been directed to Dōgo Onsen by Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto through an underwater channel. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市)}} ({{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}}, {{ill|Unzen|ja|雲仙市}}, {{ill|Obama Onsen|ja|小浜町雲仙}}, {{ill|Unzen Onsen|ja|雲仙温泉}}) - A Kokusai-mizai-sha. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|'''Overview of Shrines Related to Hot Springs'''|'''Individual Shrines Named Onsen Shrine'''|温泉神社 (曖昧さ回避)}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社, Onsen Jinja) refers to a type of {{ill|shrine|ja|神社}}, or a commonly used name, associated with {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} (hot springs).
== Overview == An Onsen Shrine is a shrine connected to or dedicated to hot springs. Such shrines are found across Japan, primarily in hot spring areas.
Japan's landscape, intersected by volcanic zones, is abundant in hot springs. Since ancient times, hot springs have been revered as the abodes of deities, known as "Hot Spring Deities" or "Onsen Kami." Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Many ancient hot springs are traditionally associated with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名神}}, who are often enshrined as Onsen Kami.
According to the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, there are three shrines specifically named "Onsen Shrine," along with others such as "Onsen Stone Shrine," "Hot Spring Shrine," "Yugami Shrine," "Miyu Shrine," and "Tamakusuyugami Shrine," making a total of ten shrines related to hot springs listed as {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}}.
== Shrines Related to Hot Springs Across Japan ==
* {{ill|Onsen Ishi Shrine|ja|温泉石神社}} ({{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Ōsaki|ja|大崎市}}, {{ill|Kawatabi Onsen|ja|川渡温泉}}) - {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}}. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōsaki)|ja|温泉神社 (大崎市)}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Ōsaki, {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Mizokuihime-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kōriyama)|ja|湯泉神社 (郡山市)}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}, {{ill|Kōriyama|ja|郡山市}}, {{ill|Bandai-Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉}}) - Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, among others. Divinity of hot springs brought from Izu by the {{ill|Azumi Itō Clan|ja|安積伊東氏}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Iwaki)|ja|温泉神社 (いわき市)}} (Fukushima Prefecture, {{ill|Iwaki|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Iwaki Yumoto Onsen|ja|いわき湯本温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|事代主命}}, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōtawara)|ja|温泉神社 (大田原市中野内)}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Kurobane Onsen|ja|黒羽温泉}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nasushiobara)|ja|温泉神社 (那須塩原市湯本塩原)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasushiobara|ja|那須塩原市}}, {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉郷}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Nasu Onsen Shrine|ja|那須温泉神社}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡}}, {{ill|Nasu|ja|那須町}}, {{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷}}) - Shikinai-sha. Famous for the {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}. The "Nasu Yumoto Daijingū" mentioned in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}} is said to refer to this shrine. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nikkō)|ja|温泉神社 (日光市)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日光市}}, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}) * {{ill|Yumae Shrine|ja|湯前神社}} ({{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海市}}, {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Izuyama Shrine|ja|射山神社}} ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, {{ill|Tsu|ja|津市}}, {{ill|Sakakibara Onsen|ja|榊原温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kobe)|ja|湯泉神社}} ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市}}, {{ill|Kita-ku (Kobe)|ja|北区}}, {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Sometimes referred to as "Onsen Shrine." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and Kumano-Kusumi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Shisho Shrine (Toyooka)|ja|四所神社 (豊岡市)}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Toyooka|ja|豊岡市}}, {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}}) - Enshrines Yuyama-no-Kami (湯山主神) and others. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Yakami-hime-no-Mikoto, Miino-Kami, and Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Tamatsukuri Shrine|ja|玉作湯神社}} ({{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市}}, {{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|ja|玉造温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Kushiakarutama-no-Kami, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yu Shrine (Dōgo Onsen)|ja|湯神社}} ({{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}, {{ill|Matsuyama|ja|松山市}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社}}. Enshrines Amenominakanushi-no-Kami, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}) - Founded during the Taishō period. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. Currently merged into {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu Tetsuwana)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, {{ill|Tetsuwana Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}}) * {{ill|Hinokuma Hime Shrine|ja|火男火売神社}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, Tetsuwana Onsen) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Hinokagutsuchi-no-Mikoto and Hiyakehayahime-no-Mikoto. It is said that {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍}} Shōnin was inspired by this shrine, called Tsurumi Gongen, to create a stone steam bath (mushi-yu). "Hayami no Yu," a hot spring flowing from the base of the sacred Tsurumidake mountain, is believed to have been directed to Dōgo Onsen by Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto through an underwater channel. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市)}} ({{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}}, {{ill|Unzen|ja|雲仙市}}, {{ill|Obama Onsen|ja|小浜町雲仙}}, {{ill|Unzen Onsen|ja|雲仙温泉}}) - A Kokusai-mizai-sha. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto.

Japan's landscape, intersected by volcanic zones, is abundant in hot springs. Since ancient times, hot springs have been revered as the abodes of deities, known as "Hot Spring Deities" or "Onsen Kami." Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Many ancient hot springs are traditionally associated with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名神|12=draft}}, who are often enshrined as Onsen Kami.
According to the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, there are three shrines specifically named "Onsen Shrine," along with others such as "Onsen Stone Shrine," "Hot Spring Shrine," "Yugami Shrine," "Miyu Shrine," and "Tamakusuyugami Shrine," making a total of ten shrines related to hot springs listed as {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}.
== Shrines Related to Hot Springs Across Japan ==
* {{ill|Onsen Ishi Shrine|ja|温泉石神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōsaki|ja|大崎市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kawatabi Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=draft}}) - {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōsaki)|ja|温泉神社 (大崎市)|12=draft}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Ōsaki, {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Mizokuihime-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kōriyama)|ja|湯泉神社 (郡山市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kōriyama|ja|郡山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bandai-Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, among others. Divinity of hot springs brought from Izu by the {{ill|Azumi Itō Clan|ja|安積伊東氏|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Iwaki)|ja|温泉神社 (いわき市)|12=draft}} (Fukushima Prefecture, {{ill|Iwaki|ja|いわき市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwaki Yumoto Onsen|ja|いわき湯本温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|事代主命|12=draft}}, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōtawara)|ja|温泉神社 (大田原市中野内)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurobane Onsen|ja|黒羽温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nasushiobara)|ja|温泉神社 (那須塩原市湯本塩原)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasushiobara|ja|那須塩原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Nasu Onsen Shrine|ja|那須温泉神社|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu|ja|那須町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Famous for the {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|12=draft}}. The "Nasu Yumoto Daijingū" mentioned in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語|12=draft}} is said to refer to this shrine. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nikkō)|ja|温泉神社 (日光市)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日光市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumae Shrine|ja|湯前神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Izuyama Shrine|ja|射山神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsu|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakakibara Onsen|ja|榊原温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kobe)|ja|湯泉神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kita-ku (Kobe)|ja|北区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Sometimes referred to as "Onsen Shrine." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and Kumano-Kusumi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Shisho Shrine (Toyooka)|ja|四所神社 (豊岡市)|12=draft}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Toyooka|ja|豊岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Yuyama-no-Kami (湯山主神) and others. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|12=draft}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Yakami-hime-no-Mikoto, Miino-Kami, and Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Tamatsukuri Shrine|ja|玉作湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|ja|玉造温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Kushiakarutama-no-Kami, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yu Shrine (Dōgo Onsen)|ja|湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuyama|ja|松山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社|12=draft}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷|12=draft}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Amenominakanushi-no-Kami, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}) - Founded during the Taishō period. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. Currently merged into {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu Tetsuwana)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, {{ill|Tetsuwana Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Hinokuma Hime Shrine|ja|火男火売神社|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, Tetsuwana Onsen) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Hinokagutsuchi-no-Mikoto and Hiyakehayahime-no-Mikoto. It is said that {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}} Shōnin was inspired by this shrine, called Tsurumi Gongen, to create a stone steam bath (mushi-yu). "Hayami no Yu," a hot spring flowing from the base of the sacred Tsurumidake mountain, is believed to have been directed to Dōgo Onsen by Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto through an underwater channel. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen|ja|雲仙市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Obama Onsen|ja|小浜町雲仙|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen Onsen|ja|雲仙温泉|12=draft}}) - A Kokusai-mizai-sha. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州|12=draft}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市|12=draft}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市|12=draft}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto.
== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=draft}}

{{Otheruses|'''Overview of Shrines Related to Hot Springs'''|'''Individual Shrines Named Onsen Shrine'''|温泉神社 (曖昧さ回避)}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社, Onsen Jinja) refers to a type of {{ill|shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}, or a commonly used name, associated with {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|12=draft}} (hot springs).
== Overview == An Onsen Shrine is a shrine connected to or dedicated to hot springs. Such shrines are found across Japan, primarily in hot spring areas.
Japan's landscape, intersected by volcanic zones, is abundant in hot springs. Since ancient times, hot springs have been revered as the abodes of deities, known as "Hot Spring Deities" or "Onsen Kami." Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Many ancient hot springs are traditionally associated with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名神|12=draft}}, who are often enshrined as Onsen Kami.
According to the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, there are three shrines specifically named "Onsen Shrine," along with others such as "Onsen Stone Shrine," "Hot Spring Shrine," "Yugami Shrine," "Miyu Shrine," and "Tamakusuyugami Shrine," making a total of ten shrines related to hot springs listed as {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}.
== Shrines Related to Hot Springs Across Japan ==
* {{ill|Onsen Ishi Shrine|ja|温泉石神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōsaki|ja|大崎市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kawatabi Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=draft}}) - {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōsaki)|ja|温泉神社 (大崎市)|12=draft}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Ōsaki, {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Mizokuihime-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kōriyama)|ja|湯泉神社 (郡山市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kōriyama|ja|郡山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bandai-Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, among others. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|事代主命|12=draft}}, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōtawara)|ja|温泉神社 (大田原市中野内)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurobane Onsen|ja|黒羽温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nasushiobara)|ja|温泉神社 (那須塩原市湯本塩原)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasushiobara|ja|那須塩原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Nasu Onsen Shrine|ja|那須温泉神社|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu|ja|那須町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Famous for the {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|12=draft}}. The "Nasu Yumoto Daijingū" mentioned in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語|12=draft}} is said to refer to this shrine. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nikkō)|ja|温泉神社 (日光市)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日光市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumae Shrine|ja|湯前神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Izuyama Shrine|ja|射山神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsu|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakakibara Onsen|ja|榊原温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|12=draft}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Yakami-hime-no-Mikoto, Miino-Kami, and Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Tamatsukuri Shrine|ja|玉作湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|ja|玉造温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社|12=draft}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷|12=draft}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Hinokagutsuchi-no-Mikoto and Hiyakehayahime-no-Mikoto. It is said that {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}} Shōnin was inspired by this shrine, called Tsurumi Gongen, to create a stone steam bath (mushi-yu). "Hayami no Yu," a hot spring flowing from the base of the sacred Tsurumidake mountain, is believed to have been directed to Dōgo Onsen by Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto through an underwater channel. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen|ja|雲仙市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Obama Onsen|ja|小浜町雲仙|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen Onsen|ja|雲仙温泉|12=draft}}) - A Kokusai-mizai-sha. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州|12=draft}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市|12=draft}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市|12=draft}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto. == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|'''Overview of Shrines Related to Hot Springs'''|'''Individual Shrines Named Onsen Shrine'''|温泉神社 (曖昧さ回避)}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社, Onsen Jinja) refers to a type of {{ill|shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}, or a commonly used name, associated with {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|12=draft}} (hot springs).
== Overview == An Onsen Shrine is a shrine connected to or dedicated to hot springs. Such shrines are found across Japan, primarily in hot spring areas.
Japan's landscape, intersected by volcanic zones, is abundant in hot springs. Since ancient times, hot springs have been revered as the abodes of deities, known as "Hot Spring Deities" or "Onsen Kami." Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Many ancient hot springs are traditionally associated with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名神|12=draft}}, who are often enshrined as Onsen Kami.
According to the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, there are three shrines specifically named "Onsen Shrine," along with others such as "Onsen Stone Shrine," "Hot Spring Shrine," "Yugami Shrine," "Miyu Shrine," and "Tamakusuyugami Shrine," making a total of ten shrines related to hot springs listed as {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}.
== Shrines Related to Hot Springs Across Japan ==
* {{ill|Onsen Ishi Shrine|ja|温泉石神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōsaki|ja|大崎市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kawatabi Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=draft}}) - {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōsaki)|ja|温泉神社 (大崎市)|12=draft}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Ōsaki, {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Mizokuihime-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kōriyama)|ja|湯泉神社 (郡山市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kōriyama|ja|郡山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bandai-Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, among others. Divinity of hot springs brought from Izu by the {{ill|Azumi Itō Clan|ja|安積伊東氏|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Iwaki)|ja|温泉神社 (いわき市)|12=draft}} (Fukushima Prefecture, {{ill|Iwaki|ja|いわき市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwaki Yumoto Onsen|ja|いわき湯本温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|事代主命|12=draft}}, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōtawara)|ja|温泉神社 (大田原市中野内)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurobane Onsen|ja|黒羽温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nasushiobara)|ja|温泉神社 (那須塩原市湯本塩原)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasushiobara|ja|那須塩原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Nasu Onsen Shrine|ja|那須温泉神社|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu|ja|那須町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Famous for the {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|12=draft}}. The "Nasu Yumoto Daijingū" mentioned in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語|12=draft}} is said to refer to this shrine. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nikkō)|ja|温泉神社 (日光市)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日光市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumae Shrine|ja|湯前神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Izuyama Shrine|ja|射山神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsu|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakakibara Onsen|ja|榊原温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kobe)|ja|湯泉神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kita-ku (Kobe)|ja|北区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Sometimes referred to as "Onsen Shrine." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and Kumano-Kusumi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Shisho Shrine (Toyooka)|ja|四所神社 (豊岡市)|12=draft}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Toyooka|ja|豊岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Yuyama-no-Kami (湯山主神) and others. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|12=draft}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Yakami-hime-no-Mikoto, Miino-Kami, and Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Tamatsukuri Shrine|ja|玉作湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|ja|玉造温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Kushiakarutama-no-Kami, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yu Shrine (Dōgo Onsen)|ja|湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuyama|ja|松山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社|12=draft}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷|12=draft}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Amenominakanushi-no-Kami, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}) - Founded during the Taishō period. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. Currently merged into {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu Tetsuwana)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, {{ill|Tetsuwana Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Hinokuma Hime Shrine|ja|火男火売神社|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, Tetsuwana Onsen) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Hinokagutsuchi-no-Mikoto and Hiyakehayahime-no-Mikoto. It is said that {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}} Shōnin was inspired by this shrine, called Tsurumi Gongen, to create a stone steam bath (mushi-yu). "Hayami no Yu," a hot spring flowing from the base of the sacred Tsurumidake mountain, is believed to have been directed to Dōgo Onsen by Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto through an underwater channel. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen|ja|雲仙市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Obama Onsen|ja|小浜町雲仙|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen Onsen|ja|雲仙温泉|12=draft}}) - A Kokusai-mizai-sha. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州|12=draft}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市|12=draft}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市|12=draft}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto. == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=draft}}

Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kobe)|ja|湯泉神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kita-ku (Kobe)|ja|北区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Sometimes referred to as "Onsen Shrine." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and Kumano-Kusumi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Shisho Shrine (Toyooka)|ja|四所神社 (豊岡市)|12=draft}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Toyooka|ja|豊岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Yuyama-no-Kami (湯山主神) and others. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|12=draft}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Yakami-hime-no-Mikoto, Miino-Kami, and Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Tamatsukuri Shrine|ja|玉作湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|ja|玉造温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Kushiakarutama-no-Kami, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yu Shrine (Dōgo Onsen)|ja|湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuyama|ja|松山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社|12=draft}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷|12=draft}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Amenominakanushi-no-Kami, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}) - Founded during the Taishō period. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. Currently merged into {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu Tetsuwana)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, {{ill|Tetsuwana Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Hinokuma Hime Shrine|ja|火男火売神社|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, Tetsuwana Onsen) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Hinokagutsuchi-no-Mikoto and Hiyakehayahime-no-Mikoto. It is said that {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}} Shōnin was inspired by this shrine, called Tsurumi Gongen, to create a stone steam bath (mushi-yu). "Hayami no Yu," a hot spring flowing from the base of the sacred Tsurumidake mountain, is believed to have been directed to Dōgo Onsen by Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto through an underwater channel. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen|ja|雲仙市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Obama Onsen|ja|小浜町雲仙|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen Onsen|ja|雲仙温泉|12=draft}}) - A Kokusai-mizai-sha. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州|12=draft}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市|12=draft}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市|12=draft}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto. == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|'''Overview of Shrines Related to Hot Springs'''|'''Individual Shrines Named Onsen Shrine'''|温泉神社 (曖昧さ回避)}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社, Onsen Jinja) refers to a type of {{ill|shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}, or a commonly used name, associated with {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|12=draft}} (hot springs).
== Overview == An Onsen Shrine is a shrine connected to or dedicated to hot springs. Such shrines are found across Japan, primarily in hot spring areas.
Japan's landscape, intersected by volcanic zones, is abundant in hot springs. Since ancient times, hot springs have been revered as the abodes of deities, known as "Hot Spring Deities" or "Onsen Kami." Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Many ancient hot springs are traditionally associated with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名神|12=draft}}, who are often enshrined as Onsen Kami.
According to the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, there are three shrines specifically named "Onsen Shrine," along with others such as "Onsen Stone Shrine," "Hot Spring Shrine," "Yugami Shrine," "Miyu Shrine," and "Tamakusuyugami Shrine," making a total of ten shrines related to hot springs listed as {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}.
== Shrines Related to Hot Springs Across Japan ==
* {{ill|Onsen Ishi Shrine|ja|温泉石神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōsaki|ja|大崎市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kawatabi Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=draft}}) - {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōsaki)|ja|温泉神社 (大崎市)|12=draft}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Ōsaki, {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Mizokuihime-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kōriyama)|ja|湯泉神社 (郡山市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kōriyama|ja|郡山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bandai-Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, among others. Divinity of hot springs brought from Izu by the {{ill|Azumi Itō Clan|ja|安積伊東氏|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Iwaki)|ja|温泉神社 (いわき市)|12=draft}} (Fukushima Prefecture, {{ill|Iwaki|ja|いわき市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwaki Yumoto Onsen|ja|いわき湯本温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|事代主命|12=draft}}, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōtawara)|ja|温泉神社 (大田原市中野内)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurobane Onsen|ja|黒羽温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nasushiobara)|ja|温泉神社 (那須塩原市湯本塩原)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasushiobara|ja|那須塩原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Nasu Onsen Shrine|ja|那須温泉神社|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu|ja|那須町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Famous for the {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|12=draft}}. The "Nasu Yumoto Daijingū" mentioned in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語|12=draft}} is said to refer to this shrine. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nikkō)|ja|温泉神社 (日光市)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日光市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumae Shrine|ja|湯前神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Izuyama Shrine|ja|射山神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsu|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakakibara Onsen|ja|榊原温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kobe)|ja|湯泉神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kita-ku (Kobe)|ja|北区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Sometimes referred to as "Onsen Shrine." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and Kumano-Kusumi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Shisho Shrine (Toyooka)|ja|四所神社 (豊岡市)|12=draft}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Toyooka|ja|豊岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Yuyama-no-Kami (湯山主神) and others. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|12=draft}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Yakami-hime-no-Mikoto, Miino-Kami, and Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Tamatsukuri Shrine|ja|玉作湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|ja|玉造温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Kushiakarutama-no-Kami, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yu Shrine (Dōgo Onsen)|ja|湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuyama|ja|松山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社|12=draft}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷|12=draft}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Amenominakanushi-no-Kami, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}) - Founded during the Taishō period. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. Currently merged into {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu Tetsuwana)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, {{ill|Tetsuwana Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Hinokuma Hime Shrine|ja|火男火売神社|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, Tetsuwana Onsen) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Hinokagutsuchi-no-Mikoto and Hiyakehayahime-no-Mikoto. It is said that {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}} Shōnin was inspired by this shrine, called Tsurumi Gongen, to create a stone steam bath (mushi-yu). "Hayami no Yu," a hot spring flowing from the base of the sacred Tsurumidake mountain, is believed to have been directed to Dōgo Onsen by Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto through an underwater channel. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen|ja|雲仙市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Obama Onsen|ja|小浜町雲仙|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen Onsen|ja|雲仙温泉|12=draft}}) - A Kokusai-mizai-sha. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州|12=draft}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市|12=draft}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市|12=draft}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto. == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=draft}}

Since ancient times, hot springs have been revered as the abodes of deities, known as "Hot Spring Deities" or "Onsen Kami." Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Many ancient hot springs are traditionally associated with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名神|12=draft}}, who are often enshrined as Onsen Kami.
According to the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, there are three shrines specifically named "Onsen Shrine," along with others such as "Onsen Stone Shrine," "Hot Spring Shrine," "Yugami Shrine," "Miyu Shrine," and "Tamakusuyugami Shrine," making a total of ten shrines related to hot springs listed as {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}.

* {{ill|Onsen Ishi Shrine|ja|温泉石神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōsaki|ja|大崎市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kawatabi Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=draft}}) - {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōsaki)|ja|温泉神社 (大崎市)|12=draft}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Ōsaki, {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Mizokuihime-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kōriyama)|ja|湯泉神社 (郡山市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kōriyama|ja|郡山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bandai-Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, among others. Divinity of hot springs brought from Izu by the {{ill|Azumi Itō Clan|ja|安積伊東氏|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Iwaki)|ja|温泉神社 (いわき市)|12=draft}} (Fukushima Prefecture, {{ill|Iwaki|ja|いわき市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwaki Yumoto Onsen|ja|いわき湯本温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|事代主命|12=draft}}, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōtawara)|ja|温泉神社 (大田原市中野内)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurobane Onsen|ja|黒羽温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nasushiobara)|ja|温泉神社 (那須塩原市湯本塩原)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasushiobara|ja|那須塩原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Nasu Onsen Shrine|ja|那須温泉神社|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu|ja|那須町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Famous for the {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|12=draft}}. The "Nasu Yumoto Daijingū" mentioned in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語|12=draft}} is said to refer to this shrine. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nikkō)|ja|温泉神社 (日光市)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日光市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumae Shrine|ja|湯前神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Izuyama Shrine|ja|射山神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsu|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakakibara Onsen|ja|榊原温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kobe)|ja|湯泉神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kita-ku (Kobe)|ja|北区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Sometimes referred to as "Onsen Shrine." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and Kumano-Kusumi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Shisho Shrine (Toyooka)|ja|四所神社 (豊岡市)|12=draft}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Toyooka|ja|豊岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Yuyama-no-Kami (湯山主神) and others. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|12=draft}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Yakami-hime-no-Mikoto, Miino-Kami, and Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Tamatsukuri Shrine|ja|玉作湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|ja|玉造温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Kushiakarutama-no-Kami, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yu Shrine (Dōgo Onsen)|ja|湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuyama|ja|松山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社|12=draft}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷|12=draft}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Amenominakanushi-no-Kami, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}) - Founded during the Taishō period. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. Currently merged into {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu Tetsuwana)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, {{ill|Tetsuwana Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Hinokuma Hime Shrine|ja|火男火売神社|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, Tetsuwana Onsen) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Hinokagutsuchi-no-Mikoto and Hiyakehayahime-no-Mikoto. It is said that {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}} Shōnin was inspired by this shrine, called Tsurumi Gongen, to create a stone steam bath (mushi-yu). "Hayami no Yu," a hot spring flowing from the base of the sacred Tsurumidake mountain, is believed to have been directed to Dōgo Onsen by Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto through an underwater channel. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen|ja|雲仙市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Obama Onsen|ja|小浜町雲仙|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen Onsen|ja|雲仙温泉|12=draft}}) - A Kokusai-mizai-sha. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州|12=draft}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市|12=draft}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市|12=draft}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto. == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|'''Overview of Shrines Related to Hot Springs'''|'''Individual Shrines Named Onsen Shrine'''|温泉神社 (曖昧さ回避)}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社, Onsen Jinja) refers to a type of {{ill|shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}, or a commonly used name, associated with {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|12=draft}} (hot springs).
== Overview == An Onsen Shrine is a shrine connected to or dedicated to hot springs. Such shrines are found across Japan, primarily in hot spring areas.
Japan's landscape, intersected by volcanic zones, is abundant in hot springs. Since ancient times, hot springs have been revered as the abodes of deities, known as "Hot Spring Deities" or "Onsen Kami." Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Many ancient hot springs are traditionally associated with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名神|12=draft}}, who are often enshrined as Onsen Kami.
According to the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, there are three shrines specifically named "Onsen Shrine," along with others such as "Onsen Stone Shrine," "Hot Spring Shrine," "Yugami Shrine," "Miyu Shrine," and "Tamakusuyugami Shrine," making a total of ten shrines related to hot springs listed as {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}.
== Shrines Related to Hot Springs Across Japan ==
* {{ill|Onsen Ishi Shrine|ja|温泉石神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōsaki|ja|大崎市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kawatabi Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=draft}}) - {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōsaki)|ja|温泉神社 (大崎市)|12=draft}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Ōsaki, {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Mizokuihime-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kōriyama)|ja|湯泉神社 (郡山市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kōriyama|ja|郡山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bandai-Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, among others. Divinity of hot springs brought from Izu by the {{ill|Azumi Itō Clan|ja|安積伊東氏|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Iwaki)|ja|温泉神社 (いわき市)|12=draft}} (Fukushima Prefecture, {{ill|Iwaki|ja|いわき市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwaki Yumoto Onsen|ja|いわき湯本温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|事代主命|12=draft}}, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōtawara)|ja|温泉神社 (大田原市中野内)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurobane Onsen|ja|黒羽温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nasushiobara)|ja|温泉神社 (那須塩原市湯本塩原)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasushiobara|ja|那須塩原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Nasu Onsen Shrine|ja|那須温泉神社|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu|ja|那須町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Famous for the {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|12=draft}}. The "Nasu Yumoto Daijingū" mentioned in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語|12=draft}} is said to refer to this shrine. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nikkō)|ja|温泉神社 (日光市)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日光市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumae Shrine|ja|湯前神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|12=draft}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社|12=draft}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷|12=draft}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Amenominakanushi-no-Kami, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}) - Founded during the Taishō period. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州|12=draft}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市|12=draft}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市|12=draft}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto. == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=draft}}
{{citation needed|Onsen Jinja}} <!--Onsen Shrines-->

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|'''Overview of Shrines Related to Hot Springs'''|'''Individual Shrines Named Onsen Shrine'''|温泉神社 (曖昧さ回避)}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社, Onsen Jinja) refers to a type of {{ill|shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}, or a commonly used name, associated with {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|12=draft}} (hot springs).
== Overview == An Onsen Shrine is a shrine connected to or dedicated to hot springs. Such shrines are found across Japan, primarily in hot spring areas.
Japan's landscape, intersected by volcanic zones, is abundant in hot springs. Since ancient times, hot springs have been revered as the abodes of deities, known as "Hot Spring Deities" or "Onsen Kami." Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Many ancient hot springs are traditionally associated with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名神|12=draft}}, who are often enshrined as Onsen Kami.
According to the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, there are three shrines specifically named "Onsen Shrine," along with others such as "Onsen Stone Shrine," "Hot Spring Shrine," "Yugami Shrine," "Miyu Shrine," and "Tamakusuyugami Shrine," making a total of ten shrines related to hot springs listed as {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}.
== Shrines Related to Hot Springs Across Japan ==
* {{ill|Onsen Ishi Shrine|ja|温泉石神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōsaki|ja|大崎市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kawatabi Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=draft}}) - {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōsaki)|ja|温泉神社 (大崎市)|12=draft}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Ōsaki, {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Mizokuihime-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kōriyama)|ja|湯泉神社 (郡山市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kōriyama|ja|郡山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bandai-Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, among others. Divinity of hot springs brought from Izu by the {{ill|Azumi Itō Clan|ja|安積伊東氏|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Iwaki)|ja|温泉神社 (いわき市)|12=draft}} (Fukushima Prefecture, {{ill|Iwaki|ja|いわき市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwaki Yumoto Onsen|ja|いわき湯本温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|ja|事代主命|12=draft}}, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Ōtawara)|ja|温泉神社 (大田原市中野内)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurobane Onsen|ja|黒羽温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nasushiobara)|ja|温泉神社 (那須塩原市湯本塩原)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasushiobara|ja|那須塩原市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shiobara Onsen|ja|塩原温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Nasu Onsen Shrine|ja|那須温泉神社|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu|ja|那須町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Famous for the {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|12=draft}}. The "Nasu Yumoto Daijingū" mentioned in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語|12=draft}} is said to refer to this shrine. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Nikkō)|ja|温泉神社 (日光市)|12=draft}} (Tochigi Prefecture, {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日光市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumae Shrine|ja|湯前神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Izuyama Shrine|ja|射山神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsu|ja|津市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakakibara Onsen|ja|榊原温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yusen Shrine (Kobe)|ja|湯泉神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kita-ku (Kobe)|ja|北区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Sometimes referred to as "Onsen Shrine." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and Kumano-Kusumi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Shisho Shrine (Toyooka)|ja|四所神社 (豊岡市)|12=draft}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Toyooka|ja|豊岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Yuyama-no-Kami (湯山主神) and others. It is said that Dōchi, who visited this shrine, was divinely inspired to open the public bath "Mandarayu." * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shirahama)|ja|温泉神社 (白浜町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishimuro District|ja|西牟婁郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shirahama|ja|白浜町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanki Shirahama Onsen|ja|南紀白浜温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Yumoto Shrine|ja|湯本神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maniwa|ja|真庭市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|12=draft}}) - The location was formerly called Yubara, but upon Maniwa City's establishment, it was renamed Yubara Onsen. * {{ill|Miyu Shrine|ja|御湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami District|ja|岩美郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwami|ja|岩美町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwai Onsen|ja|岩井温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Yakami-hime-no-Mikoto, Miino-Kami, and Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Tamatsukuri Shrine|ja|玉作湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|ja|玉造温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Kushiakarutama-no-Kami, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yu Shrine (Dōgo Onsen)|ja|湯神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuyama|ja|松山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Higashidōgo Shrine|ja|東道後神社|12=draft}} (Ehime Prefecture, Matsuyama, {{ill|Higashidōgo Onsen|ja|東道後温泉郷|12=draft}}) - A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Hio Hachiman Shrine|ja|日尾八幡神社|12=draft}}. Enshrines Amenominakanushi-no-Kami, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}) - Founded during the Taishō period. Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto, and others. Currently merged into {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Beppu Tetsuwana)|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, {{ill|Tetsuwana Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Hinokuma Hime Shrine|ja|火男火売神社|12=draft}} (Ōita Prefecture, Beppu, Tetsuwana Onsen) - Shikinai-sha. Enshrines Hinokagutsuchi-no-Mikoto and Hiyakehayahime-no-Mikoto. It is said that {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}} Shōnin was inspired by this shrine, called Tsurumi Gongen, to create a stone steam bath (mushi-yu). "Hayami no Yu," a hot spring flowing from the base of the sacred Tsurumidake mountain, is believed to have been directed to Dōgo Onsen by Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto through an underwater channel. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen|ja|雲仙市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Obama Onsen|ja|小浜町雲仙|12=draft}}, {{ill|Unzen Onsen|ja|雲仙温泉|12=draft}}) - A Kokusai-mizai-sha. Dedicated to the deity of Tsukushi Island (modern {{ill|Kyūshū|ja|九州|12=draft}}), represented in the form of "Isshin Shimen" (a single body with four faces). The shrine is also known as "Shimen-gū." The name "Unzen" derives from the reading of 温泉 (onsen), but the shrine is pronounced as written: "Onsen Jinja." Over a dozen branch shrines of the same name exist within the {{ill|Shimabara Peninsula|ja|島原半島|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Shimabara)|ja|温泉神社 (島原市亀の甲町)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Shimabara|ja|島原市|12=draft}}, Kamenokō-chō) * {{ill|Onsen Kumano Shrine|ja|温泉熊野神社|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Shimabara) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Kunimi)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市国見町多比良丁)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Kunimi-machi Tabirachō) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Azuma)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市吾妻町布江名)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Azuma-machi Fuenami) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Unzen Chijiwa)|ja|温泉神社 (雲仙市千々石町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Unzen, Chijiwa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Ariake)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市有家町山川)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, {{ill|Minamishimabara|ja|南島原市|12=draft}}, Ariake-machi Yamakawa) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Minamishimabara Kazusa)|ja|温泉神社 (南島原市加津佐町己)|12=draft}} (Nagasaki Prefecture, Minamishimabara, Kazusa-machi Mi) * {{ill|Onsen Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|温泉神社 (八代市)|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsushiro|ja|八代市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hinagu Onsen|ja|日奈久温泉|12=draft}}) - Enshrines Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto. == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=draft}}
{{citation needed|Onsen Jinja}} <!--Onsen Shrines-->
{{Onsen}}

Shrines dedicated to these deities are referred to as "Yugami Shrines" or "Onsen Shrines." Enshrines Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto. * {{ill|Yuzusame Shrine|ja|由豆佐売神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuruoka|ja|鶴岡市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yutagawa Onsen|ja|湯田川温泉|12=draft}}) - Shikinai-sha.
